params.text:
Barriers to communication

Many things can get in the way of a clear message. Being aware of them helps you reduce them:

  • Physical — noise, poor lighting, distance, PPE covering your face.
  • Sensory — hearing or sight loss, or a broken hearing aid.
  • Cognitive — dementia, learning disability, confusion or medication effects.
  • Language — English as an additional language, jargon, or complex words.
  • Emotional — pain, fear, anxiety, or feeling rushed.

Reduce barriers: cut background noise, face the person, use short sentences, allow time, and use aids the person relies on.

params.alt: Five barriers to communication: physical, sensory, cognitive, language and emotional

params.question: Mrs Patel wears a hearing aid. You notice she keeps saying "pardon?" and looks anxious. What is the best first response?

answers.text: Check her hearing aid is switched on and working, reduce background noise, and face her while speaking clearly.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct — remove the barrier and adapt how you communicate.

answers.text: Speak much louder and more slowly to everyone in the room.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Shouting distorts speech and can feel undignified.

answers.text: Write everything down instead, as talking clearly isn't working.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Writing may help later, but first check the aid and the environment.

answers.text: Carry on as normal — she will ask again if she needs to.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Ignoring the barrier risks her safety and dignity.
